pr_title: feat: rank search results by composite relevance score
pr_description: Search results were being returned in arbitrary DB order. This PR adds a multi-key ranking function that sorts by (relevance_score DESC, recency DESC, title ASC) so the best matches appear first. Includes a tie-breaking stable sort.
diff:
--- a/src/search/ranking.py
+++ b/src/search/ranking.py
@@ -1,18 +1,60 @@
 from dataclasses import dataclass
 from datetime import datetime
-from typing import List
+from typing import List, Optional
 
 @dataclass
 class SearchResult:
     doc_id: str
     title: str
-    relevance_score: float
+    relevance_score: Optional[float]  # None for unscored legacy docs
     created_at: datetime
     author: str
 
 
+def _sort_key(result: SearchResult):
+    # BUG: None relevance_score causes TypeError in Python 3 when comparing
+    # None < 0.5 is not defined — will crash on mixed scored/unscored result sets
+    return (
+        -result.relevance_score,     # DESC (negate for ascending sort)
+        -result.created_at.timestamp(),  # DESC
+        result.title.lower(),
+    )
+
+
+def rank_results(results: List[SearchResult]) -> List[SearchResult]:
+    """Return results sorted by relevance DESC, recency DESC, title ASC."""
+    # BUG: stable=True is not a valid kwarg for sorted(); it's always stable in Python
+    # The real bug: None relevance scores cause TypeError and crash the whole ranking
+    return sorted(results, key=_sort_key, stable=True)  # TypeError: unexpected keyword
+
+
+def top_k(results: List[SearchResult], k: int) -> List[SearchResult]:
+    """Return the top k results."""
+    ranked = rank_results(results)
+    # BUG: if k > len(ranked), this is fine, but if k is negative Python returns
+    # results[:-k] not an empty list — wrong behaviour for k=0 too
+    return ranked[:k]
+
+
+def deduplicate(results: List[SearchResult]) -> List[SearchResult]:
+    """Remove duplicate doc_ids, keeping the highest-scored copy."""
+    seen: dict[str, SearchResult] = {}
+    for r in results:
+        if r.doc_id not in seen:
+            seen[r.doc_id] = r
+        else:
+            # BUG: should keep higher score but uses 'or' which keeps first non-None
+            # If existing entry has score 0.1 and new has 0.9, 0.1 wins because it's truthy
+            seen[r.doc_id] = seen[r.doc_id] or r
+    return list(seen.values())
